text: The patient is positioned in lithotomy, and a topical anesthesia solution is sprayed onto the surgical area.
The surgeon injects five milliliters of solution into the subcutaneous plane of the perianal region in a circumanal manner, with 2.5 milliliters administered on each side.
The surgeon replaces the initial needle with a long spinal needle, preparing for further injection or manipulation in the perianal region.

The surgeon injects five ml of local anesthesia solution into each ischiorectal fossa, approximately six centimeters deep, without inserting a finger into the rectum or approaching the pudendal vessels. The fluid is administered centrally within the ischiorectal fossa.
The surgeon injects three milliliters of solution into the posterior presacral space, followed by an injection of two milliliters into the anterior sphincter complex.
A well-lubricated finger is inserted into the rectum, applying slight posterior traction to relax the sphincter complex, preparing for the commencement of the surgery.
